transcript.transcript: My name is Nancy Inaba and I am part of the group that's. Organizing the celebration of Idaho Watercolor Society's 50th anniversary. Today's date is September 24, 2025. I am with Pam Grant to talk about her history with IWS Idaho Watercolor Society. This oral history is conducted with the support of Idaho Historical Society as part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. The Oral History Project, of which this interview is a part, supports a commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty, and justice through meaningful community engagement and recognize, appreciate, and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Pam Grant. First, let me thank you so much for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences about Idaho Watercolor Society. Through these questions, we hope to gain insight into an interview outcome, uh, of the organization for the last 50 years. I will begin the interview with some general questions to start the conversation, and we can get into more specific topics from there. During the interview, I may adjust some of the questions to allow for a natural flow of topics, and I might ask unscripted follow up questions. Follow up questions to clarify your answer. So before we jump into the questions, do you give permission to record this interview? And can I have your you state and spell your first and last name? Yes I do. And my name is Pam Grant p a m g r a n t okay. Well, to start, can you tell me something about yourself? Okay. Well, I was born in Ontario, Oregon. My great grandparents on my mother's side homesteaded there. My grandmother was a self-taught artist. And that side of the family is very musical and artistic. And my mom plays the piano by ear. She doesn't think that's much of a talent, but she's always done crafts and painted, so I guess that's where I got it. And my dad's side of the family came to Idaho on our farm, which, um, has been over a hundred years. So I grew up in Fruitland and went to school at Fruitland High School, and they didn't have any art there. So I was the one that got to do the posters for the football games or whatever came along. Um, after after high school, I went to the College of Idaho for two years, and that's where I saw my first watercolor demo by Fred Oakey, one of Iww's members, and later on at the Art in the Park. I bought one of his paintings, so I'm going to show it to you. I think you can see it through the glass. . There's a reflection through. Your window if you push it over to your Were left. Yeah. That's pretty. You can see it. It's, you know, it's reflecting off the glass. That's good. Yeah. Get at the top. I see that. Very modern looking. Wow. Yeah. Very loose and wonderful. But it seemed magical to me that, you know, he could do that with watercolor so that I really hadn't seen that before. So that was I think the first seed was planted right there. And let's see from there, um, I married my husband and he was going to school in New York. So we spent a short time in New York City, and then we moved to Denver, where he went to law school. We have one son, and I worked so he could finish law school sooner at, um, what was a Catholic printing plant that printed all the Catholic newspapers around the country because we printed the Idaho Catholic News, and then they went out of business. So, um, I worked at the school and was a teacher's aide for, um, a few years. And then we moved back to Idaho in 1979. But in the meantime, I would take classes and paint with friends because I did some tole painting, but I took a stained glass class and that was really fun. So when we moved back to Idaho, there was an ad in the paper for a little stained glass company on Broadway that wanted a stained glass artist to help them, because they had a commission for 50 stained glass windows at Saint Mark's Catholic Church. So they hired me. And, you know, I had no professional experience, but my cutting got really good in that year because they had a year to get those 50 windows done. Did you design the windows? They did. Yeah. The, owner and his wife. It was their business. And she was the designer. Mhm. You know I just cut all the little pieces. I think that really has influenced I can still see that coming out in my artwork today because I love shapes and I'm, I'm an abstract painter. So I think that's kind of where it came from. Interesting. Let's see. Um, so after we moved to Boise, I worked in, um, clerical again in a small, um, office supply business they repaired. Do you remember what a typewriter was? They did that. Sold and repaired typewriters and and, uh, I started taking a watercolor class from Doctor Peck at BSU at night, so that was a lot of fun. So that, I think, was the next step in my, my journey. And Larry, um, was working at micron and that's when they were, you know, up and down and it looked like they weren't going to go out of business. So, um, I went back to school full time and got my art degree, and that's about the time I joined AWS, which is around probably 1988. And, um, I let's see, I, I think I was southwest rep and then I know I was corresponding secretary there, and I took a lot of wonderful workshops from whoever came in and I was studying from Dwight Williams, one of our members. So I've got one of Dwight's, and this one doesn't have a reflection. Yeah, that comes across beautifully. Yeah. Isn't that gorgeous? It is. So Dwight was a big influence in such a wonderful man. Um, I think we've all learned a lot from him. We miss him. So let's see. Um, after that, um, we moved. Larry got a job in California. Knew it was just a temporary two year project. So we moved to San Jose, and I, um, took a lot of classes there, too, because people loved to come there in the winter time. And, uh, yeah. So that was a fun experience. And I took from Dale Laitinen and he said, you should be an abstract artist. So he kind of, you know, pushed me down that path. So I'm still going down that path. Um, and, um, that was about the time that our two grandsons were born in Redmond, Washington. So we moved there after Larry finished his project. And, um, I got involved with the Northwest Watercolor Society, and that's probably my most productive time because I had an outside studio. I worked a lot harder than I'm working now, but that's okay, because, um, you know, that was just when I had I was young and had energy, but I got my signature from the Northwest Watercolor Society in 2002 and was in met a lot of wonderful people there. And in fact, I still keep in touch with a couple of my friends. They've moved out of the area. But you know, we keep in touch. So that's the one thing about any watercolor society. I moved around a lot and joined them is the people you meet and the fun you have at projects and, and workshops. It's um, um, you know, it's been really the most important part of my life, I could say, other than my family. But, um, let's see then in it was about 2003, we moved back to Fruitland. My mom was having some problems and, uh, we moved back to our farm there, and I rejoined AWS so I could keep painting and getting involved with activities there. I, um. Um. Let's see. What did I do there? I know I was vice president, and that was a fun job because you got to select the juror for our jury chose. That was, um. Um, big job. And then in 2008. Well, no, I did show I was a show chair to, uh, one year. And I'll talk about that, uh, later, because there's so much work in that particular position. It's a year round job of just things happen each month that you have to keep on top of. And then I was president for the Watercolor Society in 2008 to 2010. Wow. And I think keeping membership is probably the biggest challenge and keeping people interested. Um, of, um, one thing I noted the society has done is we have different categories. If you've been in so many shows, you get a, um, another name to your your, uh, your what is it, the. Uh, the distinguished member. Distinguished member. Yes. And if you. Yes, that's what it is. Um, so just keeping membership. Um, I think one thing they've done recently is join with the Plein Air Group, which is good too, because so many of our members were members of the Plein air. And, uh, we all have things in common. We like to paint no matter where it's at. Um, when I was president, we I think one challenge we had, of course, it always is, is money. and our annual workshops. We would bring in a juror from out of state and pay their expenses and send them to North Idaho for a workshop, and they would do one here in the Treasure Valley area in Boise and then over in East Idaho. Well, those weren't paying for themselves and the outside regions. So what we decided to do during that time was alternate. One year we'd go north and the next year they'd go to the east. So I would drive the juror, and it was fun to meet the juror. Uh, you know, you can talk with them and figure out what their what they like to do and how they. The one lady was president of the Florida Watercolor Society. So she was interesting on how their membership worked. So, um, that was one plus. Um. Then, um, I was thinking about what to explain how the Idaho Watercolor Society really works. In September, we have our annual meetings, and, um, that's usually one place where we can all get together. We elect new officers and, um. Have fun and paint. And usually there's a workshop to. Sometimes they have a show. So that kind of starts off our year. And then in October, that's really when the show starts being prepared, our annual juried show. And we need to get a prospectus out. So the person that does that, um, will We'll get that out to all of our memberships. And then as the entry date comes due, they collect all the images. Now it's done through, um, like art call or one of those. But you're still busy organizing the traveling show, which our society will send the paintings from Boise to Twin Falls and Pocatello and Idaho Falls, and then up to Moscow. Sometimes it's in a different, different, um, routine. It just kind of depends on how how it works with their venues. So, um. And then when the juror comes in, it's just entertaining them. They usually have a three day workshop. Um, I know it was looking at Skip Lawrence, he said. We worked him to death when he had to do. Off. So it's probably easier on the juror to to just have two workshops to do. While they're here. Um, the first of the year, uh, is when we have our rotunda. Show at the state capitol. And that's open to all of our members. Um, and you don't have to be juried into that. You just enter and then bring your paintings. Um, then after that, June is our big, busy month with, uh, juried show. And after it's here for a month, then it travels around the state. So, um. Then we usually take a break in the summer, and then it kind of all starts over again. But, um, it's been really fun. I think probably the one challenge that a president faces is, um. How to keep maybe the regions happy. And I know we had a problem with the North region because they wanted to use AWS name and to sell paintings, and we didn't want to lose our non-profit status, because you can't do that when you're a non-profit. So we kind of agreed to disagree. And they, uh, you know, they're so far away. It's they are so close to Spokane and can join up activities there, even up in Moscow and Lewiston and Clarkston. But I think it's been within, what, the last two New Years or two years that we've been allowing members them from out of state if they border the like Clarkston and then probably Ontario could if members there and then up in Coeur d'Alene area. So I think that's helped to um, our juror this year was from Coeur d'Alene or not our at our annual meeting, our workshop coordinator. Right. Yeah. She, um, I'm sure did a great job. I wasn't able to go this year, but I met her at the the Western Fed of Watercolor Society, um, meeting. She's a neat gal and a wonderful artist. So that brings me to the Western Fed. What? That is when I was, um, in the Northwest Watercolor Society, I felt like Idaho could really use being around a bigger group. That might be the next step for our our more experienced members that wanted the challenge of of entering bigger, juried shows if they were ready to take that step before, maybe they did like Iowa or NWS or AWS, which are the two largest national shows in this country. So, uh, we had an invitation from, um, New Mexico, who was in charge that year to join. And I thought, well, that's a good idea. We'll do that. Um, so it went to the membership and they voted on doing that. And what it is is Arizona, which is Tucson area, and Colorado, Idaho, New Mexico, San Diego and then southern areas. No. Southern. Excuse me. It's, um. Uh, southern Arizona Watercolor society, so it's, um, um, Tucson and then Phoenix area. I've got those two mixed up, um, Utah, the Watercolor Society of Oregon and West Texas, which is and the Southwestern Watercolor Society is in the Dallas area. So, uh, it I've been to most of those, and it's so much fun to see how they present their show and the facilities they have, which some of them are just fabulous and, um, just being experiencing watercolor from different parts of the country so that we host would were to host in 2020. Um, and we had when I was in school, I had done an internship at the historical Society, and so I took a prospectus to them of, of, um, what we wanted to do, and they accepted our show. So we were planning on having our show in 2020 at the Idaho Historical Museum. And um, Anne Sorensen was our chair, and she was so good about organizing people. And when we talked about, you know, what was important about this show, we thought it'd be more important for people just to get to know each other. So we started having monthly get togethers and potlucks. Everybody would bring something and then we'd tell them the progress. And of course, we were looking for more workers too, because we knew it would take a lot of work to get it done. But it was fun just to be around and meet people that I hadn't met before. Yeah. So that went on for a couple of years before the show. And, um, Steven Quiller was our juror, and he had juried all the paintings. And we had them in an studio to get it, get them ready to frame. And then that's when the lockdown came. So we juried or he he judged all the paintings for the awards and we were able to give those out after things had kind of settled down in June at the Riverside, We set the paintings out. We didn't frame anything. We just had them all set out and people could put their mask on and walk through and see the paintings. So and we presented the awards and, and then sent them back to the artist. But that was probably one of our most trying times was, you know, just having to change things and working so hard for something because we really had planned a lot of fun things for people to do. But I think that the good thing that came out of it, I think we're still using some of that. We're giving demos, you know, like our annual meetings, which other societies have done. So, um, yeah. Uh, looking back on that, I just thought, oh boy, we get to do that again good. In And 2030. Yeah. Any questions for me? Um, yeah. I know that was quite a challenging time because IWS is a small society with not a lot of membership. Yeah. It was a challenge to raise the money, to get the venues, to have everybody so excited. And then who would have thought a worldwide pandemic would have taken the whole thing down? Right. Well, one thing about our society, too, you know, the other regions and I didn't really mention how we're how we work. We have five different regions. That's Idaho Falls, Pocatello area and Twin Falls, and of course, Boise. And then up in, um, the Moscow Lewiston area and up north, too. It's just so hard for them to get together. Um, and they have other groups there too that they work with, but, um, they're giving given a percentage of their memberships dues so they can have parties and paint outs and get togethers and like Connie Pepper in, in, um, Twin Falls, I, I don't know, has it been 25 years that she's been the rep, but they get together almost weekly to paint. They've got a great group there. And I know Idaho Falls does too. Right. So, um, yeah. It is a challenge for a statewide organization to keep some degree of cohesion when geographically you're so separated. Yeah. I know Colorado. When when after we had joined, they asked me about how we did this because they were talking about doing the same thing. So we do share ideas with um, the other societies. Mhm. Yeah. Yeah. That makes a lot of sense. Um, well one of the I mean, you touched on a lot of the questions that I had. Um, are you still doing watercolor or have you branched out into other mediums? Oh, I should, um, I'll have to show you my painting. This one I did in 2006 and and got the jurors choice award. And I'm going to duck here so you can see a little bit of it. And boy, I've got a reflection on that too, don't I? Um, there's a truck across the street. In a nice car just went by. Yeah. Yeah, I see what my neighbors are doing. They're doing some landscaping over there. And what do you call that painting? What's the title? That's grandmother's shawl. And, um, I started it's just got lots of layers. And that's what I like to do now is I. I'm not a purist, and I add watercolor and acrylic and inks and I love these layers you can build up. And that's what that was. I was even doing that then. And then the face was my great grandmother came out of it. So I'm more of an intuitive painter now. And that's kind of the road I'm going down. Um, plus, I just like to play with paint, you know, and if I have to be that restricted to say, you know, save this and don't paint that, you know, it just this makes me happy the way I do. Um. Um, big mess and then try to figure out how to get out of it. I appreciate, the workshop that you gave on abstracts. It has changed the way I do things now, and I'm enjoying it a lot more. Have you done a lot of teaching? I haven't, you know, and I all of you were such good guinea pigs because I'm giving another class next week. Um, and in Weiser. And I know that class will be better just because I practice on all of you. But no, I enjoy teaching, but I don't do enough of it. It's, you know, it's really an art in itself because I've taken so many workshops and watching people that that teach, and one of my most favorite artists doesn't demonstrate, you know, and I think that's she is so good and I love her work so much. But I would love to see her paint. So yes, I think it's hard to be in that mindset of painting and talking and tending to people's needs all at the same time, it is a different mindset. I watched it, uh, demo through northwest watercolor society last night, and he didn't. He had his paper drawn out, but he had painted it before. And then he came in and, uh, talked about what he was doing. And that would be doable because it's just two different mindsets. You know, when when I'm painting, I like to get lost in what I'm doing. You know, time will disappear. This is my time. And I can just immerse myself in what I'm doing. And the rest of the world, you know, isn't out there. And I think that's when the creativity starts to flow, too. So I, um. Yeah, that's the way I like to. That's what I'm doing now. Who knows what will come next? Because I think one thing kind of leads to another. Mhm. Yeah. It's interesting. Um, can you say anything more about creativity? Okay. Um. Mm. That's such a hard question because to get to that point where you're not feeling inhibited, you know, whatever comes out is okay. And I think that's what kind of creativity is, because I think we're so afraid of making a mistake or messing something up. Um, I think it's just being really I like the looseness, you know, that happens when when you just kind of let it let it happen and, and then you figure out, you know, do you have a good design, and that's kind of like the puzzle, putting the puzzle back together after you've gotten. So I don't know whether I really answered that creativity. That's that's a hard one. Yeah, I think sometimes, there's a lot of emphasis on technique and process, but not so much talk of creativity. But really it's it is a hard one to explain because it's such an individual process. Yeah it is. And my art background is advertising and, and um, you know, that was very restrictive, except the last year I took, uh, Experimental Watercolor Society or water color painting class, and that was fun. We just did all kinds of silly things, you know, like putting your paper in the freezer and get some frost on it, and then throw the paint on it. And, um, you get wonderful effects if you can get that right. Temperature. But um, so, but you want to be able to if I want to paint a person or a dog or something, I want it to look like a dog and I want the person. So that just takes, I think, hand-eye coordination. You have to just train your brain and your hand to work together. Um. But but real creativity, I think, comes from within our experiences and how you feel at the moment. Or maybe you're really disgusted at something, you know, that can come out too. So yeah. Yeah. Yeah. An expression of your inner landscape. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Whatever it might be. Well, do you have an outfit like a story that stands out in your mind about your long time with AWS? Oh, yeah. I was going to, you know, I think that goes back to when I first joined. I met a lot of people, and Ruth Clark, I can remember going to her house. She lived in this tiny little carriage house. It was beautiful. Set back in the big trees on Warm Springs Avenue. And when a juror would come, she would have a big picnic for them and entertain people at her house. She was such a wonderful person to. She just loved giving because she had a lot of students that would stay at her house that couldn't afford, you know, the housing fees. So she would host them. And, um, we would go to Bogus Basin with AWS, too, and have a winter paint out or kind of an off season. It wouldn't be when they're skiing. But, um, that was always fun because we would stay up there. And one other time we went up to, um, McCall because that I think at the time we were there was owned by the University of Idaho, and they rented out their cabins, and we had our annual meeting and workshops. And, you know, it's this time of year in October when it's so beautiful up there. The lake is still and the trees are gorgeous. So, um, we've gone to a lot of places. One other time we went to Hagerman, which was fun, out to Thousand Springs because they have a pool there, and we did the same thing. We had a workshop and everybody got to paint and we camped out and, um, yeah. Those sound like really fun activities and are really a way of getting to meet each other too. Oh it is. Yeah. Yeah. Other than when you're working with somebody, you, um, you know, you're chatting and and then you have lunch together and, you know, you become friends. So. Yeah. Yeah. It is interesting how the social parts are the outstanding memories for many people. Yes. Sure. Because if you wanted to be a serious artist, you know, that takes a lot of dedication. Um. And I can't say I'm that serious. I'm serious about my painting. I want to get better. But as far as being out there and selling and, you know, making a living at it, I don't want to do that. I'm fortunate that I don't have to do that at this stage in my life. Mhm. So yeah. Yeah, there's many aspects to being an artist and the commercial side is a whole different set. Yes it is. Yeah. You have to work really hard at that. I admire people that do that because it's not the easiest career. It's not. And it's, um, a different social skill set for artists. Mhm. It is. How does one become a salesperson. Yeah. Right. Yeah. Um, yeah. So I understand that you're at a local gallery now. Mhm. I just joined the Artsource Gallery and um, in fact when I think they started in 93. So they've been going a long time and been in lots of different locations. Lou Ann Lee was one of. She did wonderful workshops. She was an AWS member. Her and her husband, John. He was a veterinarian and he did woodworking. So they got people together and started this little gallery. Dwight was one I think Joyce was working full time, so I don't think she was. And we'll have to ask her. But, um, I did belong to that until we moved to California. So now that I'm back, I've just rejoined. And they thought they needed an abstract artist. So I thought, well, it's a beautiful it's a beautiful gallery, so it'll be fun to meet some new people again. And I love the variety of the artwork that they have there. Um. I did. Yeah. Yeah. Hopefully they will do well in that location because it's sure pretty pretty Gallery. Yeah, they're doing a lot of things to that area of Boise to attract people. Yes. Last time I was down, they just removed all the barriers. So the street lights of the stoplight was working. So they won't have that to contend with, hopefully. Yeah. Well, I failed to mention earlier in your introduction that you are part of, um, this our task force to, um, create the history for IWS. And, um, your memories and connections are vital to give this project meaning. Well, this has really been fun as far as I could turn my computer around and show you the boxes I have in the corner, but I won't do that. Just going through the old scrapbooks and, you know, it brings back memories of people that are long gone and that I've known. So, yeah, it's been a good journey down memory lane. Yeah, it's been fun. Yeah. Isn't that wonderful to be able to look in the rear view mirror and see all the people and the experiences that you've had? Yeah. Oh, because this organization couldn't have done all the work it did, because all the workshops and all putting together a show is so much work and it's, you know, just keeping an organization going for 50 years, I think is amazing. But it's just taken a lot of dedicated people, and it's been fun to look at their faces again in the scrapbooks and newsletters and things that I've been going through. We've done a lot of volunteerism for IWS. What, um, motivated you to to donate so much time and effort? Um, well, it's given me a lot, as far as, you know, helping me on my artistic journey. Um, plus good friends, you know, around people you like to be with because you're sharing the same things. Um, I don't know. I can't think of anything else I'd rather be doing. Okay, that's a good answer. Um, sounds better than cleaning the bathroom or, you know. Well, sometimes I use those things as avoidance. Yeah. True. That's true. Um, is there anything else that you'd like to add? Um, I don't know whether I probably left something out, but I, um. I think that's it. Other than I want to thank you for being our fearless leader, because you've worked out and kept us going in this project. I think it's going to be fantastic, and everybody's going to enjoy getting together and seeing the history of IWS, because they will have the same reaction I've had when they see pictures of people they remember. It's a rich history, and I'm hoping that we will gain some insight about what's important in the organization, like the outings and the relationships and the fun that you had. Yeah, we need to promote those things. Yeah. For the future. That's what I'm getting from these interviews, how important that is. Yeah. Yeah it is. And I know that's what Anne and I were talking about, too, with, uh, Western Fed. I know we have the critiques now which are really well attended, you know, and it's a learning process, especially for beginning painters or people that are really, you know, are having problems with something and want to find out why. But, um, yeah, the social aspect is really important. Um, and I'm sorry I missed Stanley this year because that would have been nice going up. It's a beautiful location. Yeah. Um. And it's a good idea that they're trying to do it in different areas, too, because it is hard for the people to the east to always come over here. And we know that the north. That's even harder. Well, we have a beautiful state and it's great. We highlight the different regions. Yeah. And get us all out of our bubbles. True. Yeah. Okay, well, thank you so much for your time, Pam. And all the service that you've given IWS. I mean, you have all this history because you've you've given so much. And I'm glad you've gotten something. Yes. Yeah, it really was the beginning journey for me. Uh, I with all the workshops that I was able to take and the people I learned from, like Dwight and, you know, many others. Yeah. There's a amazing individuals so generous with their time and energy and skills. Yeah, that is a unique person. Yeah. Yeah. So nice that we have a whole organization of many people like that. Yeah. Well, like you. Goodness, yes. Because you've kept us on the straight and narrow here on on this, keeping this going. This will be something I know people will enjoy. I've really enjoyed learning about it. It's been, um, far more than I was anticipating. Yeah. Yeah, it has been. Hasn't it? Kind of grew like a mushroom. Yeah. Okay, I'm going to push finish and review. And if, um, you'll, I think be able to see the transcript. So if there's anything that we want to edit then we can edit. But I don't see anything that's that stands out in my mind. I think I stumbled over the Arizona in, Phoenix and Arizona. But that's the only thing probably I remember seeing because I didn't want to leave anybody out. That's right. Okay. Thanks so much, Pam. Bye bye. Bye.

lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose of the Interview

> Nancy Inaba introduces herself and explains the context and purpose of the interview, which is to document the history and experiences of the Idaho Watercolor Society as part of its 50th anniversary and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. She outlines the goals of the oral history project, the structure of the interview, and obtains Pam Grant's consent to record and participate.

metadata.title: Introduction and Purpose of the Interview
metadata.synopsis: Nancy Inaba introduces herself and explains the context and purpose of the interview, which is to document the history and experiences of the Idaho Watercolor Society as part of its 50th anniversary and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. She outlines the goals of the oral history project, the structure of the interview, and obtains Pam Grant's consent to record and participate.

lines.line: # Pam Grant's Early Life and Artistic Influences

> Pam Grant shares her family background, describing her artistic and musical heritage, particularly on her mother's side. She recounts her upbringing in Fruitland, Idaho, her lack of formal art education in high school, and her early exposure to art through family and school projects. Pam also discusses her initial college experience and the pivotal moment when she witnessed her first watercolor demonstration, which planted the seed for her future artistic pursuits.

metadata.title: Pam Grant's Early Life and Artistic Influences
metadata.synopsis: Pam Grant shares her family background, describing her artistic and musical heritage, particularly on her mother's side. She recounts her upbringing in Fruitland, Idaho, her lack of formal art education in high school, and her early exposure to art through family and school projects. Pam also discusses her initial college experience and the pivotal moment when she witnessed her first watercolor demonstration, which planted the seed for her future artistic pursuits.

lines.line: # Early Career and Move Back to Idaho

> Pam describes her family's long-standing roots in Idaho and her early adult life, including marriage and moves to New York and Denver. She details her work experiences, such as working at a Catholic printing plant and as a teacher's aide, and her ongoing interest in art through classes and crafts. Upon returning to Idaho in 1979, she takes a stained glass class, which leads to a job creating stained glass windows for a local church, an experience that influences her later abstract art.

metadata.title: Early Career and Move Back to Idaho
metadata.synopsis: Pam describes her family's long-standing roots in Idaho and her early adult life, including marriage and moves to New York and Denver. She details her work experiences, such as working at a Catholic printing plant and as a teacher's aide, and her ongoing interest in art through classes and crafts. Upon returning to Idaho in 1979, she takes a stained glass class, which leads to a job creating stained glass windows for a local church, an experience that influences her later abstract art.

lines.line: # Entry into Watercolor and Idaho Watercolor Society

> Pam recounts her transition from stained glass to watercolor, including her night classes at Boise State University and her decision to pursue an art degree. She describes joining the Idaho Watercolor Society around 1988, her early roles within the organization, and the influence of mentors like Dwight Williams. Pam also shares her appreciation for the workshops and the supportive community she found in AWS.

She describes joining the Idaho Watercolor Society around 1988, her early roles within the organization, and the influence of mentors like Dwight Williams. Pam also shares her appreciation for the workshops and the supportive community she found in AWS.

lines.line: # Moves, Further Involvement, and Artistic Development

> Pam discusses her family's move to California for her husband's job, where she took additional art classes and was encouraged to pursue abstract painting. She then moves to Washington to be near her grandchildren and becomes active in the Northwest Watercolor Society, earning her signature status and forming lasting friendships. Pam emphasizes the importance of community and the personal growth she experienced through her involvement in various watercolor societies.

Pam emphasizes the importance of community and the personal growth she experienced through her involvement in various watercolor societies.

lines.line: # Return to Idaho, Leadership Roles, and Organizational Challenges

> Pam returns to Fruitland, Idaho, to care for her mother and rejoins AWS, taking on leadership roles such as vice president, show chair, and eventually president from 2008 to 2010. She reflects on the challenges of maintaining membership and engagement, the importance of recognizing distinguished members, and the benefits of collaborating with other art groups. Pam also describes the logistical and financial challenges of organizing workshops and shows across the state.

Pam also describes the logistical and financial challenges of organizing workshops and shows across the state.

lines.line: # Structure and Operations of Idaho Watercolor Society

> Pam outlines the annual cycle and organizational structure of AWS, including the election of officers, the preparation and execution of the annual juried show, and the logistics of the traveling exhibition. She explains the roles involved in organizing these events, the process of hosting jurors and workshops, and the importance of the Rotunda Show at the state capitol. Pam highlights the year-round nature of these responsibilities and the collaborative efforts required to keep the society running smoothly.

She explains the roles involved in organizing these events, the process of hosting jurors and workshops, and the importance of the Rotunda Show at the state capitol. Pam highlights the year-round nature of these responsibilities and the collaborative efforts required to keep the society running smoothly.

lines.line: # Regional Issues, Membership, and Western Federation Involvement

> Pam discusses the ongoing challenges of maintaining membership and balancing the needs of different regions within AWS. She describes specific issues, such as the use of the AWS name for sales and the need to protect the organization's non-profit status. Pam also details AWS's involvement with the Western Federation of Watercolor Societies, the process of joining, and the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on planned events, including the cancellation and adaptation of a major show.

Pam also details AWS's involvement with the Western Federation of Watercolor Societies, the process of joining, and the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on planned events, including the cancellation and adaptation of a major show.

lines.line: # Regional Organization and Social Cohesion

> Pam elaborates on the regional structure of AWS, which includes five distinct areas across Idaho. She explains how each region receives a portion of membership dues to support local activities and describes the challenges of fostering cohesion in a geographically dispersed organization. Pam highlights the importance of regular gatherings, painting sessions, and the sharing of ideas with other societies to maintain a sense of community and support among members.

Pam highlights the importance of regular gatherings, painting sessions, and the sharing of ideas with other societies to maintain a sense of community and support among members.

lines.line: # Artistic Practice, Teaching, and Creativity

> Pam shares her current artistic practice, which involves mixed media and an intuitive, layered approach to painting. She discusses her enjoyment of abstract art, her experiences teaching workshops, and the challenges of balancing demonstration with personal creativity. Pam reflects on the nature of creativity, emphasizing the importance of letting go of inhibitions, embracing mistakes, and drawing on personal experiences and emotions in her work.

lines.line: # Memories, Stories, and Social Aspects of AWS

> Pam recounts memorable experiences from her time with AWS, including gatherings at members' homes, paint-outs at scenic locations, and the camaraderie developed through shared activities. She emphasizes the significance of social interactions and friendships formed within the society, noting that these relationships are often the most cherished aspect of her involvement. Pam also reflects on the balance between artistic seriousness and the enjoyment of community.

lines.line: # Commercial Aspects and Gallery Involvement

> Pam and Nancy discuss the challenges of the commercial side of being an artist, including the skills required for marketing and sales. Pam shares her recent involvement with the Artsource Gallery in Boise, her history with the gallery, and her role as an abstract artist. They talk about the variety of artwork in the gallery and the efforts to attract visitors, highlighting the differences between professional and hobbyist approaches to art.

lines.line: # AWS History Project and Reflections on Volunteerism

> Pam discusses her participation in the AWS history project, the process of reviewing old scrapbooks and newsletters, and the value of preserving the organization's legacy. She reflects on the importance of volunteerism, the personal rewards she has gained from her involvement, and the dedication required to sustain AWS over five decades. Pam expresses gratitude for the friendships and artistic growth she has experienced through her volunteer work.

lines.line: # Closing Remarks and Final Reflections

> Nancy and Pam conclude the interview by reflecting on the rich history of AWS, the importance of social activities and relationships, and the lessons learned from the organization's past. They discuss the ongoing efforts to document and celebrate AWS's legacy, express appreciation for each other's contributions, and share their hopes for the future of the society. The conversation ends with mutual gratitude and a sense of accomplishment for their collaborative work.

children.text: # Introduction and Purpose of the Interview

> Nancy Inaba introduces herself and explains the context and purpose of the interview, which is to document the history and experiences of the Idaho Watercolor Society as part of its 50th anniversary and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. She outlines the goals of the oral history project, the structure of the interview, and obtains Pam Grant's consent to record and participate.
# Pam Grant's Early Life and Artistic Influences

> Pam Grant shares her family background, describing her artistic and musical heritage, particularly on her mother's side. She recounts her upbringing in Fruitland, Idaho, her lack of formal art education in high school, and her early exposure to art through family and school projects. Pam also discusses her initial college experience and the pivotal moment when she witnessed her first watercolor demonstration, which planted the seed for her future artistic pursuits.
# Early Career and Move Back to Idaho

> Pam describes her family's long-standing roots in Idaho and her early adult life, including marriage and moves to New York and Denver. She details her work experiences, such as working at a Catholic printing plant and as a teacher's aide, and her ongoing interest in art through classes and crafts. Upon returning to Idaho in 1979, she takes a stained glass class, which leads to a job creating stained glass windows for a local church, an experience that influences her later abstract art.
